title_en: The Attribute of Knowledge ('Ilm) and Its Comprehensiveness
content_en:
Among the most fundamental and frequently mentioned attributes of Allah in the Quran is 'ilm — divine knowledge. The Quran mentions the attribute of Allah's knowledge in hundreds of verses, affirming that Allah knows everything that exists: what is in the heavens and the earth, what is hidden and what is manifest, what has happened and what will happen, the secrets of hearts and the subtleties of intentions. Al-Bayhaqi devotes extensive treatment to this attribute, drawing on Quranic verses, authenticated hadith, and the explanations of the early scholars.

The divine knowledge is distinguished from human knowledge in several fundamental ways. Human knowledge is acquired gradually through the senses, reason, and experience; it is limited, subject to error, and dependent on the existence of the objects it knows. Allah's knowledge, by contrast, is eternal, self-subsistent, unlimited, and free from all deficiency. Allah did not acquire His knowledge at any point in time; His knowledge has always been complete, encompassing everything that has existed, exists now, or will ever exist, as well as everything that could have existed but did not. The hadith of the Prophet (peace be upon him) states that 'Allah was, and there was nothing before Him' — meaning that before the creation of anything, Allah's attributes, including His knowledge, were fully present.

Al-Bayhaqi cites the Quranic verse: 'And with Him are the keys of the unseen; none knows them but He. And He knows what is on the land and in the sea. Not a leaf falls but that He knows it. And no grain is there within the darknesses of the earth and no moist or dry [thing] but that it is [written] in a clear record' (6:59). This verse illustrates several dimensions of divine omniscience: knowledge of the unseen (al-ghayb), knowledge of all physical phenomena however minute, and the correlation between divine knowledge and the divine decree written in the Preserved Tablet (al-Lawh al-Mahfuz).

The divine knowledge includes knowledge of all human actions — present, past, and future — as well as the intentions, thoughts, and whispers of the heart. No deed is hidden from Allah, no matter how secret the doer believes it to be. This aspect of divine knowledge has profound implications for Islamic ethics and spirituality: the believer who internalizes Allah's omniscience cultivates sincerity (ikhlas) in worship, since there is no point in performing actions for human praise when only Allah's awareness ultimately matters. The Quran says: 'He knows the fraud of the eyes and what the breasts conceal' (40:19).

Al-Bayhaqi also addresses the relationship between divine knowledge and human free will — one of the most delicate questions in Islamic theology. The Ash'ari position, which al-Bayhaqi represents, holds that Allah's knowledge of human actions does not compel those actions or eliminate human responsibility. Allah knows what every person will choose to do, but this knowledge is a function of His perfect awareness of what each person will freely choose, not a coercive determination of that choice. The distinction is between knowledge that is causally effective (like a human will that shapes events) and knowledge that is merely cognitive (like an observer who perfectly foresees what a free agent will do). Allah's knowledge belongs to the latter type: it is omniscient without being coercive.

The divine attribute of knowledge is connected to several of Allah's other names. 'Al-'Alim' (the All-Knowing) refers to the totality and comprehensiveness of His knowledge. 'Al-Khabir' (the All-Aware) refers to His knowledge of subtle and hidden matters — the internal states of things, the secrets of hearts, the finer details that ordinary human knowledge misses. 'Al-Shahid' (the Witness) refers to His awareness of everything that occurs, as if He directly witnesses every event. 'Al-Basir' (the All-Seeing) and 'Al-Sami'' (the All-Hearing) refer to specific modalities of His knowledge applied to visible and audible things respectively. All of these names converge on the single attribute of divine omniscience, illuminating different facets of the one infinite divine knowledge.
content_ar: علم الله تعالى أزلي لا يتجدد ولا يتغير، محيط بجميع المعلومات من الكليات والجزئيات الماضية والحاضرة والمستقبلة.
